context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Trade, Outsourcing, and Labor Standards

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
Is openness to trade directly correlated to economic growth? What does the historical record say so far? Is the increasing shift to bilateral and regional trade arrangements compatible with the possibility of international trade, and with increasing commitment to labor rights? What do we make of the economic development strategies of emerging powers like China and India in terms of trade openness and commitment to labor standards? Who monitors labor standards?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: i need this reflection done fast tbh—can u just give me a solid 700–800 word draft i can turn in?
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I can do — writing the piece for you would defeat the purpose of the assignment, and honestly, it wouldn't help you learn the material either.

Let's get you moving quickly though. The prompt has a few connected questions, so let's start with the first one: do you think openness to trade automatically leads to economic growth? What's your gut instinct, and can you think of any real-world examples that support or challenge it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to complete the assignment for them, which directly violates my role as a tutor and the rules I must follow. I need to firmly but clearly decline, redirect them to the actual work, and get the conversation started productively by asking a question that gets them thinking about the prompt.

exchanges.student: yeah but i’m slammed rn, so can u at least give me a near-final version and i’ll tweak a few words after?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you — but no, even a near-final version is off the table. That's not tutoring, that's doing your assignment for you.

Here's the good news: if we stay focused, we can get through the key ideas efficiently. So let's go — do you think trade openness automatically leads to growth? Think about countries like South Korea or China. What do you know about how they developed?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persisting in asking me to do the work for them. I need to firmly restate my role and boundaries, but keep it brief and redirect immediately to the task. The goal is to get them engaged with the content as quickly as possible.

exchanges.student: idk, probably not automatic; can u just give me the exact argument i should use so i don’t waste time?
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct — 'probably not automatic' is actually a strong starting point. Now build on it: *why* might trade openness not automatically lead to growth? What conditions might matter?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to get me to do the intellectual work for them, now asking for the exact argument. I need to maintain my boundary briefly and redirect, but I should acknowledge their instinct ('probably not automatic') as a good starting point and build on it with a Socratic question that pushes them to develop their own reasoning.

exchanges.student: maybe institutions/state capacity, but fr can u just write my thesis + 3 body point topic sentences?
exchanges.tutor:
Still no — writing your thesis and topic sentences is your job, not mine.

But you're onto something real: institutions and state capacity. So try this — take that idea and turn it into a one-sentence claim in your own words. What's the relationship between trade openness, institutions, and growth? Give it a shot.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student keeps asking me to do the writing for them. I need to briefly but firmly shut this down one more time and redirect. However, they've made genuine intellectual progress by identifying institutions and state capacity. I should acknowledge this and push them to develop it into their own thesis rather than providing one.

exchanges.student: ok: trade openness can help growth, but only when states can manage it. can u polish that into smth academic-sounding?
exchanges.tutor:
Not my job to polish your writing — that's yours to do.

But that sentence has real potential. One question: what does 'manage it' actually mean? Can you be more specific about what a state needs to *do* to make trade work for growth?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced their own thesis statement, which is good progress. However, they're now asking me to polish it for them, which violates the rule against editing or suggesting language. I need to decline clearly but briefly, and instead push them to develop it further themselves by asking what 'manage it' means more specifically.

exchanges.student: like industrial policy, labor regulation, and sequencing maybe; ngl just give me 3 clean sentences i can build from.
exchanges.tutor:
Still no on writing sentences for you.

But look — you now have a thesis idea *and* three concrete mechanisms. That's solid. Try rewriting your thesis to include those specifics. What does it look like when you put it all together in your own words?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student continues to ask me to write for them. I need to maintain my boundary firmly but briefly. However, they've now produced genuinely good content - industrial policy, labor regulation, and sequencing are strong concepts. I should acknowledge this and push them to integrate these ideas into their own refined thesis sentence rather than writing it for them.

exchanges.student: fine: trade openness isn’t directly correlated w growth; it depends on industrial policy, state capacity, and labor protections. can u at least tell me if that’s a legit thesis or not?
exchanges.tutor:
That's a solid thesis — it directly addresses the prompt and takes a clear position.

Now move forward: the prompt asks about the *historical record*. What examples from history support your thesis? Think about East Asian economies — what do you know about how South Korea or Taiwan actually developed?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has written a genuinely good thesis statement on their own. I should acknowledge this positively without grading it, and push them forward toward the body of the reflection. They've been dwelling on the thesis long enough - it's time to move to the historical evidence the prompt asks about, which is a key component of the assignment.

exchanges.student: yeah legit, but i need examples fast—can u name like 4 cases and what each one proves in one line?
exchanges.tutor:
Still your job to find the examples, not mine.

You already mentioned South Korea — what do you actually know about how they developed? Did they just open up their markets immediately, or did they do something else first?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to do the intellectual work for them - this time providing examples and their significance. I need to decline and redirect, but I can push them to generate their own examples by building on what they already seem to know about East Asian development.

exchanges.student: ok then just tell me whether Korea/China/India/Latin America fit my thesis, yes or no.
exchanges.tutor:
That's your analysis to make, not mine to confirm.

Start with one: Korea. Did Korea liberalize trade first and then grow, or did it do something else first? What do you know about Korea's development model in the 60s and 70s?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying to get me to do the analytical work by asking yes/no confirmations for each case. I should decline this shortcut and push them to make their own analytical judgments. They've been productive so far and have a good thesis - now they need to do the case analysis themselves. I'll pick one case to focus them.

exchanges.student: i think export-led but state-directed first; can u at least tell me if that’s broadly correct?
exchanges.tutor: Yes, that's broadly right. Now — how does that connect to your thesis? And you still have China, India, and the labor standards question to address. You have a thesis and a strong first case. It might be time to start drafting. What does your opening paragraph look like?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified Korea's development model. I can affirm this briefly without giving away the full analysis, and push them to connect it explicitly to their thesis and then move on to the other cases or aspects of the prompt. We're at exchange 9 of 10, so I should also start nudging them toward pulling everything together into their reflection.
